title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-15 11:00 UTC
text:
🟢 Progressing — architecture-hardening sweep clears five merges this hour on clean CI, including one data-corruption-class fix that had merged uncaught before.

USER: NIP-02 follow/unfollow now behaves identically on iOS and Android — apps built on nmp get consistent social-graph state cross-platform, today.

DEVELOPER: nmp is still two nouns — live queries and write intents; describe intent, the engine owns relays, subscriptions, canonical state. Newly adoptable: RelayIngestResult now embeds the canonical CommittedMutationResult instead of hand-copying its fields, so relay-ingest and local-accept share one invalidation path.

THIS HOUR — merged: #498 (arch-review guardrails + cross-SDK parity script), #499 (Kotlin NIP-02 parity), #500 (mutation-result unification), #501 (test-relay teardown state), #497 (live-store reset guard — the corruption fix). In review: #502 (typed FFI error taxonomy), #503 (bool-lifecycle refactors) — both green except swift-package still pending.

VERIFIED: the #490 epic's review named this violation class after one instance merged uncaught elsewhere; all five merges ride green CI (fmt, clippy, workspace tests) plus #498's new cross-language surface governance; #501 swaps a locale-fragile string match for real closed-state truth.

PERFORMANCE: build farm spiked to 14.78 one-minute load on 8 cores (likely this session's CI); 15-min average 4.79 is more typical. Public surface holds near 29.7k of the 30k-line ceiling.

NEXT HOUR: #502's typed FFI errors and #503's bool-lifecycle types are expected to land — unblocking the queued #491 signer/FFI cleanup burst behind them.

title: NMP Briefing — 2026-07-15 11:30 UTC
text:
WHAT NMP IS — NMP (Nostr Mobile Primitives) is an embeddable Nostr client engine — a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin SDKs — that owns the machinery apps otherwise rebuild badly: the event store, relay connections, subscription routing, canonical state, signing, and durable publishing. You talk to it with two nouns: a live query (a declarative demand observed through your platform's native reactive primitive) and a write intent (a durable publish obligation tracked through a reattachable receipt). It is a library you call, not a framework you live inside.

WHAT IT CAN DO TODAY
• Reading & state: declarative live queries with reactive bindings ($currentPubkey, derived projections, set algebra). A canonical redb-backed store handles provenance-preserving dedup, replaceable events, NIP-40 expiry (event-driven), kind:5 deletion with tombstones, and supersession with exact negative-delta invalidation. Snapshots return rows plus scoped evidence — never a global 'synced'/'complete'.
• Relays & networking: full connection lifecycle with one finite fan-out ceiling (local/private/.onion rejected by default). NIP-65 outbox routing is self-bootstrapping — configure only indexer relays and the engine discovers each author's write/inbox relays. Parse-once typed ingest with bounded parallel signature verification. NIP-11 metadata and NIP-77 negentropy sync are wired. NIP-42 AUTH participates but its evidence states are still partial (experimental).
• Signing & identity: local keys and full NIP-46 remote signing (bunker) — reconnect across store close/reopen, bounded sign-only path across all four surfaces. Gap: Keychain/Keystore providers, NIP-55, per-write identity override, secret zeroization.
• Publishing: durable write intents — Accepted is one atomic persistence boundary (frozen body, receipt, pending row visible to queries), then signature promotion, cancellation/compensation, and a persisted outbox with bounded retry. At-most-once ambiguity becomes OutcomeUnknown, never a blind resend.
• Protocol modules (opt-in): NIP-02 following (Swift + Kotlin), NIP-29 groups, NIP-51 relay-list reading with guarded favorite-relay editing landing now, an optional content module and SwiftUI components. Not built: NIP-25 reactions, general draft composition, media/Blossom.
• Storage & diagnostics: crash-safe redb store (binary rows, indexes, interned relay URLs), in-memory store for tests, a destructive reset that refuses to delete a live store. Diagnostics are a permanent proof surface.
• Platforms: Rust core is the truth. Swift SDK qualified on macOS host (iOS-Simulator runtime test target pending). Kotlin SDK is desktop-JVM only — no Android AAR qualified yet.

HOW SOLID IT IS — 100+ Rust test modules, differential falsifiers against an independent store, live-relay tests, cross-SDK parity. CI runs fmt, clippy, workspace tests, both package builds, and a trusted-base surface-governance gate. Sharp edges: an M5 iOS main-thread jank whose root cause is fixed but not re-measured on device; NIP-29 storage wins verified as microbenchmarks not device room-open; AUTH evidence states, cross-process reset exclusion, and full boundedness still open.

PERFORMANCE — Built for bounded memory and streaming. Busiest-room query 5.15ms → 0.26ms; rejected-heavy search 0.188ms → 0.005ms; derived-set resolver over 59,915 rows 3,786ms → 0.73ms. NIP-11 cache has a proven ~67 MiB raw-body ceiling. Public facade 29,734 lines against a 30,000 ceiling. CI green.

WHERE IT'S HEADING — Govern the provisional shapes toward a v2 freeze; encode lifecycle invariants as types; close platform qualification (iOS-Simulator target, Android AAR); standard secure-storage signer providers; finish bounded delivery with explicit shortfall.

THIS HOUR — Merged: #502 (typed FFI error taxonomy for NIP-11/NIP-46) and #503 (two bool lifecycle gates re-encoded as Reservation/AsyncWait types). In flight, both green: #504 (session-executor ownership as a SessionExecutor type) and #488 (guarded NIP-51 favorite-relay editing).

title: NMP — The Audio Tour: What It Is & How Solid It Is
text:
A conversational, listen-first tour of NMP — not the README read aloud. ~3.5 min.

So, what is NMP, and why should you care? You're building a Nostr app. The wire protocol looks tiny — a handful of event kinds, some relays, done. And then reality shows up: relay discovery, outbox routing, subscription repair, dedup, replaceable events, deletions, expiry, retries. And the awkward question nobody answers honestly — did the network actually give me everything, or am I just guessing? Most apps rebuild all of that, and most get it subtly wrong.

NMP is the bet that you shouldn't have to. An embeddable Nostr client engine — a Rust core, with Swift and Kotlin SDKs on top through UniFFI. A library you call, not a framework you move into: your app keeps its own architecture, screens, and identity. NMP owns the network machinery underneath.

It comes down to two nouns. A live query: you describe what you want, NMP keeps a local view fresh and re-routes relays as inputs change, observed through your platform's own reactive primitive. A write intent: you describe something to publish and how durable it must be, NMP carries it through acceptance, signing, routing, retries, and per-relay outcomes — and reports what it actually saw on the wire, not a cheerful success-equals-true.

What it can do today: self-bootstrapping outbox routing (hand it two indexers, it discovers each author's write relays); a remote-signer bunker that survives reconnect across a store close and reopen; a durable outbox with bounded retry that reports outcome-unknown instead of blindly resending; and negentropy set reconciliation with a passing live test where the engine finds an event it never asked for.

How solid: the Rust core is genuinely proven — 100+ test modules, differential falsifiers, live relay tests. Busiest-room query 5.15ms to about 0.26ms; a resolver over ~60,000 rows from almost four seconds to under a millisecond. Bounded memory and streaming, not first-N truncation.

Honest gaps: pre-1.0. Swift qualified on the macOS host, iOS-simulator target pending; Kotlin desktop-JVM only, no Android package; content-relay AUTH doesn't answer the challenge yet; NIP-51 lists are read-only; reactions and media aren't built.

Where it's heading: freeze the v2 surface, encode lifecycle rules as types, close the platform gap, ship secure-storage signer providers. The known-gaps doc names every open item. Own the hard machinery, and don't lie about what's proven. That's NMP.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-15 19:00 UTC
text:
Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin SDKs on top, built so your app doesn't have to reinvent relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing from scratch. Since the last update, the team landed a solid batch of hardening work, and one fix is worth pausing on.

Start with the serious one: a DNS rebinding style SSRF hole in relay admission (#524). The engine checked a hostname's resolved IP against its local-and-private blocklist, then reconnected later and resolved that same hostname again — and DNS can answer differently the second time. That gap is closed. Admission now pins the resolved IP it already approved and connects to that exact address, so a relay can't pass the safety check once and then quietly point somewhere internal on the next connection.

Alongside it, a critical transport bug (#511): a relay that permanently fails — say, it demands auth and never accepts it — used to wedge forever and leak a connection slot. That's fixed, and the send queue behind it is now bounded instead of growing without limit.

There's bounded-memory work too. The router's coalescing pass (#510) used to scale roughly cubically with plan size; it's now quadratic, same output plan, just faster at scale. Two bookkeeping structures — one tracking attribution shapes on every recompile (#512), one tracking negentropy backfill state and per-relay event counts (#514) — used to grow unboundedly under long-running connections; both are now pruned as they go.

On cross-platform parity: Kotlin's content session can now be paused non-terminally with a proper stop() call (#523), matching what Swift already had. And Swift and Kotlin's NMPConfig now actually expose the max-relays ceiling and the allowed-local-relay-hosts list (#513), instead of silently dropping them.

One more: NIP-29 groups no longer emit an unsafe previous-tag reference that could point at stale state (#516); it's omitted rather than shipped wrong.

Bigger picture: there's an open architecture decision (#485) to stop adding a parallel "History" set of types for paginated queries, and instead make windowing a policy on the one existing observe call — an optional window parameter rather than a whole second vocabulary. Not shipped yet, but that's the direction the read side of the API is heading.

So: a real SSRF fix, a wedge-prone relay bug closed, three unbounded-growth points pruned, tighter Swift-Kotlin parity, and a cleaner read-API on the way.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-15 19:53 UTC
text:
Since the last update, one thing needs saying plainly: master went red for a bit, and here's exactly why.

Last hour's fix for a D N S rebinding hole in relay admission (#524) was correct on its own terms — it pins the resolved I P address a relay was approved on, instead of trusting a fresh D N S answer that could point the same hostname somewhere internal later. But it also flipped a default: local and loopback relays are now rejected unless an app explicitly allows them. The Swift and Kotlin test suites both lean on local relay fixtures for their integration tests, and those fixtures got caught by the same net. It merged anyway, and master's Kotlin package lane went red the moment it landed — a security change shipped without checking the two S D K lanes it would break. The good news: caught fast, fixed fast. A follow-up hotfix (#528) explicitly admits the local relay fixtures those tests need; both lanes are green again, and the security posture itself is untouched.

Right behind that, three more hardening changes landed within the hour. #525 indexes the wake-relay lookup by relay instead of scanning the whole outbox on every wake-up. #526 adds secondary indexes to the in-memory store and batches its garbage-collection pass, continuing the same bounded-memory push from the last batch. And #527 is about honesty: the documentation has been claiming kind ownership — which module owns which event kind — is enforced, and an audit this week found it isn't; there was zero enforcement. That's now closed for real, with a claim-set fold and a dedicated audit crate. One more is still in continuous integration as of this recording: #529 rounds out account lifecycle — there's long been a way to add an account across Rust, F F I, Swift, and Kotlin, but no symmetric way to remove one, and this adds remove-account across all four surfaces.

Now the bigger architectural move in flight. The read side of the A P I is being reworked, under #474 and #485, so paginated history stops being a second, parallel concept beside the ordinary live query. Today there are effectively two ways to read — the normal observe call, and a separate History-shaped noun bolted on just for pagination, with its own continuation token. The design in progress retires that second noun entirely: you call the same observe with a query and an optional window, and windowing becomes a policy on the one read noun, not a parallel vocabulary. Not shipped yet — this is in-progress design work — but it's where the read side is converging, because two nouns quietly doing overlapping jobs is exactly the kind of thing that diverges later.

So, in one line: a security fix's blast radius on C I got caught and patched rather than left red, three more hardening changes landed right behind it with one more queued, and the history-versus-window redesign keeps moving as the next real shape change to the A P I.

title: NMP Briefing — 2026-07-15 20:55 UTC
text:
Hey, it's the NMP briefing. This one's a bigger one — we're due for a deeper look this time, so let's take our time.

The headline this session is big, and it's the good kind of big: reading in NMP just got simpler, not more complicated. For a while, NMP had two ways to ask for data — a live query, for "give me updates forever," and a separate concept called History, for "give me a page of the past." Two nouns doing overlapping jobs, and that eventually drifts out of sync and gets weird at the edges.

So the team deleted History as its own thing. There's one read noun now — observe, called with a query and an optional window — and windowing is just a policy you attach to it. Ask without a window and you get an unbounded live feed of deltas, forever. Ask with a window and you get a bounded, authoritative snapshot instead. That's not an arbitrary product choice — it's a correctness constraint. An unbounded feed that tried to deliver full snapshots on every change would get quadratically expensive as the result set grows; a bounded window can afford snapshots precisely because it has a ceiling. So delivery mode isn't a separate setting anymore — it's derived from whether you bounded the query, closing off a whole class of "wait, is this a live feed or a page?" confusion.

One more detail: hitting the edge of your window used to be awkward to represent. Now there's an explicit fact called AtBound that tells you, plainly, "you've reached the edge of what you asked for." It's not an error and it's not a null — it's a first-class piece of information your app can react to on purpose.

This change also fixes a real bug living quietly in the scrolling path. Scrolling deep into history was leaking a relay subscription on every page advance, and if you scrolled far enough, you could pile up dozens of open subscriptions per relay and trip its connection limits. That's fixed now — a deep scroll holds a small, constant number of live subscriptions per relay, no matter how far back you go. It's also a good process story: CI actually caught a leak in the native task handling on the new path before it shipped broadly, someone fixed it, and there's now a regression test guarding that exact spot. Three related issues, all closed by one coherent redesign instead of three separate patches.

Alongside that flagship change, four smaller but real fixes landed this session too. A same-day hotfix unbroke the Swift and Kotlin package builds after an earlier security change made local relay connections opt-in by default — the right call, it just needed a quick follow-up. The outbox's wake-relay lookup now indexes by relay instead of scanning the whole outbox on every check. The in-memory store picked up secondary indexes and batched garbage collection. And kind ownership — the rule about which module is allowed to write which kind of event — is now actually enforced in code, instead of being true only by convention. That's the sort of gap that's easy to overlook until somebody accidentally crosses it.

Now, what's still ahead. The next big piece of protocol work is authentication on content relays — a relay requiring proof of who you are before it'll serve certain data, not just before you publish. Today that only really works on the signer connection, when you're talking to a remote signer; on ordinary content relays it's still early days. What's just gotten underway is the first wave of a seven-part plan to build a properly scoped notion of session identity, so a public anonymous reader and two different logged-in accounts on the same relay stay completely separate instead of quietly mixing. It's real design work just starting — not a shipped feature, so don't expect authenticated content reads today.

And one thing that's intentionally on hold: there's a draft for cleanly removing an account — fully detaching a signing key instead of just deactivating it — but it's paused on purpose, until it can be reshaped to match the account-handle design coming out of that authentication work. Better to wait a beat than build the same thing twice.

That's the briefing. A genuine architectural win this time, a handful of quiet-but-real fixes, and one important thing deliberately waiting its turn. Thanks for listening.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-15 21:56 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse. Two things this hour: one real merge with hard numbers, and one honest look at foundation work still mid-flight.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin SDKs, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

First, the merge. Up to now, NMP had good evidence that a large local cache answers queries fast, but no real proof it could become a large cache under actual relay load without falling over. That gap just closed. A new end-to-end harness pushed one million genuinely signed events through the real path -- websocket framing, signature verification, bounded transport queues, the engine, all the way into the redb store -- and every frame was accounted for and came back exactly right after a full shutdown and reopen. Throughput held around forty-three hundred events per second, ninety-fifth-percentile apply latency under five seconds, store size around two gigabytes. The same shape of test on the pre-hardening code managed sixty-three events per second and didn't even finish a ten-thousand-event run in five minutes. That's a real, measured floor under a number this project has been claiming for a while.

Here's the honest part, though: proving it also found the next problem. During that million-event run, the process held onto around six hundred and thirty megabytes at peak, and most of that didn't come back after shutdown. On a phone, that's the kind of pressure that gets a process killed. So alongside the proof, a new issue is now open to attribute exactly where that memory is going and get it bounded, before this becomes a mobile-scale claim instead of a desktop one. That's the project's ethos in one sentence: don't just claim it, measure it, and when the measurement finds a new problem, write it down instead of hiding it.

Second, quieter story: the content-relay authentication work, wave one of a seven-part plan, keeps moving. The piece that scopes identity to a session -- so an anonymous reader and two different logged-in accounts on the same relay can never quietly bleed into each other -- just passed an adversarial review built to try to break that isolation. It held, sound, no leaks. It's now in the build lane: the linter's clean, but the full workspace gate caught a real mismatch between how a write's status is reported directly from Rust versus through the FFI boundary Swift and Kotlin talk to. Two different tellings of the same fact -- exactly what a facade can hide if nothing's watching. It's being fixed now, before merge, alongside Swift and Kotlin wrapper updates for the new identity variant. Still pre-merge, still engine-internal, no app-facing AUTH API yet, but real progress on the actual foundation.

Meanwhile, remove-account stays exactly where it was: drafted, paused, waiting for that same session-identity work to settle first.

That's the pulse -- a real scale proof with the numbers to back it, an honestly-reported memory gap right behind it, and quiet, careful progress on the harder authentication foundation underneath.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-15 22:51 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse. No new merge this hour -- master sits exactly where it was after last hour's relay-ingest scale proof. But there's a genuinely interesting story happening just behind that line, worth walking through, because it's process working exactly the way it's supposed to.

The content-relay authentication work -- proving who you are to a relay before it'll serve certain data, not just before you publish -- is a seven-part landing plan, and wave one is the foundation: giving every session a properly scoped identity, so an anonymous reader and two different logged-in accounts on the same relay can never quietly bleed into each other. That piece already passed its adversarial review, a review built specifically to try to break that isolation. It held. Sound, no leaks.

But passing that review didn't mean it was done, and this hour shows exactly why. Once the work moved into the full workspace gate -- the one that also builds Swift and Kotlin, not just Rust -- it caught a sequence of real issues the focused review never touched.

First, a parity divergence between how a write's status gets reported directly from Rust versus through the FFI boundary Swift and Kotlin talk to. Two different tellings of the same fact. Fixed now, in the conservative direction: the write plane reverts cleanly back to public status, and authenticated write sessions get deferred to a later wave instead of shipping half right.

Second, the new access-context variant needed a Swift SDK test updated just to compile against it. Also fixed -- small, but exactly the kind of thing a Rust-only review can't see.

Third, and this is the one worth pausing on: a transport regression where session-keyed admission -- the new logic deciding which relay connections get access under the new identity model -- stopped honoring the local-host allowlist. Plainly: a relay that should have been allowed to connect, because it was explicitly permitted as a local loopback relay, wouldn't connect at all. Here's the interesting part -- that wasn't caught by a dedicated auth test. It was caught by the ingest smoke test that came out of last hour's million-event scale proof, a completely different piece of proof infrastructure, built for a different reason, catching a regression in a feature it wasn't even testing for. That fix is in progress right now.

So, nothing merged this hour, but don't read that as nothing happening. This is what a security-sensitive refactor is supposed to look like: land it in careful, reviewed waves, keep the parity oracle and the full cross-language gate running the whole time, and let them catch the kind of subtle, cross-boundary bug that one rigorous review alone can miss. All of this stays pre-merge and engine-internal -- there's no app-facing AUTH API yet, and there won't be until this wave lands.

Meanwhile, remove-account stays exactly where it's been: drafted, held, waiting on this same work to settle its account-handle shape first.

That's the pulse -- no merge to report, but real, careful progress on the hardest, most security-sensitive piece of work in flight, caught being careful in exactly the way you'd want it to be.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-15 23:54 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse. Big one this hour: the piece that's been building for the last two pulses just merged.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin SDKs, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

The headline: wave one of content-relay authentication is in. It's the first real brick of a bigger structure, so let's be precise about what it is, and just as important, what it deliberately isn't.

Here's the problem it solves. Every relay connection in NMP has always been identified by its URL. Fine when everyone's anonymous. But once a relay can tell who's asking -- a reader, your account, someone else's account -- identifying a connection by URL alone gets dangerous. If a public session and two accounts ever shared the same plan or socket bookkeeping, work fetched under one identity could get silently credited to another. Not hypothetical; it's a named item on this project's own bug-class ledger.

The fix: relay identity is no longer just a URL, it's a session -- a URL paired with an access context, either public or a specific account. Everything downstream keys off that pair instead of the bare address: which plan a piece of demand belongs to, its subscription IDs, attribution and coverage evidence, and how admission counts against the relay ceiling. Two accounts talking to the same relay are now structurally two separate sessions, not by convention, but because the types won't let them collide.

And this didn't merge on faith. It went through an adversarial review built to break that isolation -- six invariants checked with file-and-line evidence, covering context distinctness, no residual cross-credit, clean disconnects, session-counted admission, and write-path consistency. All six held, clean.

Now, the honest part, because it'd be easy to oversell this. There's no challenge from the relay yet, no response, no state machine proving a session's identity. Writes still ride the plain public session by design -- authenticated writes are deferred rather than shipped half-correct. There's no "log in to a relay" call an app can make today. What exists is the identity plumbing authentication will need once it exists, proven safe first, because retrofitting it after real credentials are flowing would be scarier.

Content-relay authentication is planned as three waves. This is wave one, done. Wave two, just started, is the actual authentication reducer -- tracking a relay's challenge, sending the signed response, separating a plain write acknowledgement from genuine proof of identity. Real protocol work, still ahead.

One small honest note: a flaky macOS test in the mock relay transport got filed as its own issue -- testing-infrastructure quirk, not a product bug.

And remove-account stays exactly where it's been: drafted, held, waiting for this same work to settle.

So, in one line: the first real brick of content-relay authentication is laid, it passed a genuine adversarial test, and it deliberately can't log anyone in yet. That's the pulse.

title: NMP Briefing — 2026-07-16 01:58 UTC
text:
Hey, it's the NMP briefing. Let's zoom out and walk the real arc of the last several sessions, not just this hour's diff.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin SDKs, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch. Two nouns: a live query for reading, a write intent for publishing.

First, a quick recap of the read side, the last big shape change before this arc. Reading used to be two overlapping ideas -- an ordinary live query, and a separate History concept bolted on for paginated results. That second noun is gone. There's one read call now, observe, taking a query and an optional window: unbounded gets you a live feed of deltas forever, bounded gets you an authoritative snapshot with an explicit AtBound fact at the edge. That redesign also closed a real subscription leak in the deep-scroll path. Settled, shipped, moving on.

Behind that, a genuine hardening sweep landed. A DNS rebinding hole in relay admission was closed by pinning the resolved address a relay was actually approved on. A permanently-failing relay used to wedge a connection slot forever with an unbounded send queue behind it; both fixed. The router's coalescing pass went from scaling roughly cubically with plan size to quadratically, same output. Two bookkeeping maps that grew without limit under long-running connections are pruned now, the in-memory store gained secondary indexes and batched garbage collection, and kind ownership -- which module may write which event kind -- went from an unenforced convention to something the type system actually checks. Most recently, the store's page cache got an explicit sixty-four megabyte ceiling, cutting peak memory on a million-event ingest run by eighty-five percent. None of these are flashy alone; together, they're the difference between claiming boundedness and measuring it.

Now, the real arc: authentication on content relays. It's the headline story across the last several sessions, best told as one throughline, because the whole point is that it's landing in deliberate, reviewed waves instead of one big unreviewable drop.

The problem: every relay connection used to be identified by nothing more than its URL. Fine when everyone's anonymous. But once a relay can tell who's asking -- a reader, your account, someone else's account -- identifying a connection by URL alone gets dangerous, because work fetched under one identity could get silently credited to another.

Wave one, merged a couple sessions back, fixed the identity half: relay identity became a session -- a URL paired with an access context -- so two accounts on the same relay are now structurally separate sessions, not by convention. It passed a six-invariant adversarial review clean: context distinctness, no residual cross-credit, clean disconnects, session-counted admission, write-path consistency, all held.

Wave two is this session's real headline, and it's the harder half: the actual authentication reducer and its epoch state machine, where a relay's challenge and an app's signed response become real, typed protocol state instead of a placeholder. There's now a frozen event template for the authentication response -- its id commits to every field, so mutating it afterward breaks either id equality or signature verification. A relay's authentication-ok response stays structurally separate from an ordinary write acknowledgement, so the two can never be confused. And authenticated write sessions -- reverted to plain public ones back in wave one, deliberately -- are reintroduced properly: a protected write parks on real authentication state for its exact session, while an unauthenticated write sends optimistically, gets told it needs authentication, and parks rather than failing or duplicating.

This wave went through an eight-invariant adversarial review that actually found something: a missed-wakeup bug, where a write waiting on authentication could get stranded across a disconnect, reconnect, or restart and never get re-driven -- the worst bug class on this project's own ledger. Fixed now, with a generation-scoped fallback and a boot-time safety net, verified with dedicated counterfactual tests. Re-review found nothing left unresolved.

One more honest detail: this wave wanted to expose per-session authentication state in diagnostics, and the project governs its public surface under a hard line-count ceiling so growth can't sneak in unreviewed. Rather than blow past that ceiling or ship a half-finished cross-language projection, the team raised the ceiling by a reviewed, explicit amount for the Rust-side diagnostics this wave, and deliberately deferred the same read-out for Swift and Kotlin to wave three, so no language's surface promises more than it delivers. The governance rule forced a decision instead of a workaround.

So: two of the three planned waves are merged. Wave three, still ahead, is the capstone -- binding this state to the real runtime and proving it end to end over an actual WebSocket connection to a real relay. Said plainly: content-relay authentication still does not ship an app-facing API today, there's no call an app can make to log in to a relay yet. What exists is a proven, reviewed foundation underneath where that call will eventually live -- and not shipping unproven claims is the whole point.

Two smaller notes. A flaky macOS-only test in the ingest smoke suite got its own tracking issue -- a timing quirk in a hand-rolled mock relay server, not an engine or transport bug, with Linux CI staying authoritative meanwhile. And remove-account, fully detaching a signing key instead of just deactivating it, stays exactly where it's been -- drafted and intentionally held until it can be reshaped around the account-handle model this same authentication work is defining.

That's the briefing: the read-model redesign settled, a hardening sweep proven with real numbers, and the authentication arc's hardest wave landed clean, with one deliberately-scoped piece left for next time. Thanks for listening.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-16 02:52 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin SDKs, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

Nothing new merged on master this hour, but there's real movement worth flagging: pull request 542, wave three of content-relay authentication, is sitting in its final continuous-integration run right now and could land at any moment.

Quick reminder of the shape so far. Wave one gave every relay connection a properly scoped session identity instead of just a URL. Wave two built the actual authentication reducer -- the challenge, the epoch state machine, the frozen auth-event template, authenticated write sessions reintroduced. Both are merged, and both passed adversarial review clean.

Wave three is the capstone, and it's a big one: runtime capability binding, plus a real-WebSocket authentication proof against an actual relay. Not a mock -- a genuine end-to-end run where the engine receives a real challenge, applies its authentication policy, signs the response, sends AUTH, gets back OK, issues a subscription request, receives end-of-stored-events, and gets real rows back. All the way through.

It doesn't stop at the happy path either. The same capstone proves a denial gets parked correctly instead of dropped or blindly retried, that a reconnect gets a genuinely fresh challenge instead of reusing a stale one, and there's a dedicated wrong-challenge oracle -- a test that deliberately signs against the wrong challenge to prove the fixture would actually catch a broken implementation, not just one that happens to pass.

All eight lifecycle and leak invariants this wave checks passed adversarial review, with no correctness holes found.

Here's the honest framing, because it matters. This is engine-level proof, not an app-facing feature yet. Waves one through three prove that authenticated content-relay reads genuinely work end to end at the engine layer. Still ahead, under a later wave: exposing that through the actual software development kit -- a real app-facing AuthPolicy call, authentication diagnostics an app can read, and reconciling the still-held remove-account work, PR 529, which needs that same account-handle shape to land first.

So: the biggest piece of authentication work in this whole arc is one green check away, not merged yet as of this recording. Check back next hour for whether it landed.

Two smaller notes, unchanged from last time. A flaky macOS-only test in the ingest smoke suite, tracked separately as its own issue, is a testing-infrastructure quirk, not a product bug. And remove-account stays exactly where it's been -- held on purpose until the account-handle shape it depends on settles.

That's the pulse. Nothing merged, but the biggest authentication milestone in this arc is sitting right at the finish line.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-16 04:53 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

Big news this hour: pull request five forty three merged. That's wave five of content-relay authentication -- the wave that makes authentication something an application can actually call, not just something the engine proves to itself.

Here's what landed. An application can now register an authentication policy through the stable Rust interface. It can add an account and get back a proper account-registration handle, and remove an account by that same handle -- replacing the old pubkey-only shape that pull request five twenty nine used, which is why that one was closed as superseded. That also closes out issue four ninety five for good. A session now exposes its own authentication diagnostics too, so an app can see what's happening instead of guessing.

Worth calling out specifically: doing this the naive way -- re-exporting the engine's authentication types straight through the facade -- would have blown through the thirty-thousand-line ceiling this project holds itself to. Instead, every one of these types is facade-owned: defined fresh at the boundary, converted explicitly, at roughly one line each. Net effect: the governed public surface didn't grow to pay for this feature, it shrank, staying well under the ceiling. No exception granted, no guardrail moved. An independent adversarial review checked mirror-conversion completeness, exact-instance registration semantics, and strict delegation of the pending and cancel machinery, and passed all three clean.

So here's where that leaves the authentication arc. Engine-level proof landed a few hours ago: a real WebSocket relay, a real challenge, a real authenticated read, end to end. This hour, the Rust application interface for that same capability landed too. What's left -- the final wave, wave six -- is projecting it out through the foreign-function interface into Swift and Kotlin, so a native iOS, Android, or desktop app can actually call it. That work is in flight right now.

The honest framing hasn't changed in substance, just in scope: the whole thing is real, and every wave has been adversarially reviewed. What's missing is the last mile -- wiring it out to the platforms people actually ship apps on. Until that lands, only the NIP-46 remote-signer authentication path is shippable end to end today.

One smaller, unchanged note: there's still a flaky macOS-only test in the ingest-smoke suite, tracked separately as infrastructure, not a product bug.

That's the pulse. NIP-42 came in through the front door this arc -- no shortcuts, no guardrail bypass -- and next hour's story should be the first native platform seeing this authentication surface directly.

title: NMP Briefing — 2026-07-16 05:54 UTC
text:
Hey, it's the NMP briefing. Time to zoom out, because this is a big one: the headline this session is that content-relay authentication is complete, end to end, and issue eight -- the umbrella tracking all of it -- is closed.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

Let's walk the arc, because the story isn't just that it shipped -- it's how it shipped. Six waves, delivered this session, every single one adversarially reviewed before it was allowed to land.

Wave one gave every relay connection a properly scoped session identity instead of just a URL -- closing a real structural gap where two accounts on the same relay could quietly bleed into each other's credit. Clean identity-isolation review.

Wave two built the actual authentication reducer: the challenge, the epoch state machine, a frozen event template for the auth response, authentication-ok kept structurally separate from an ordinary write acknowledgement. Here's where the discipline shows -- the adversarial review on this wave found a real missed-wakeup bug, a write that could get stranded waiting on authentication across a disconnect or restart and never get re-driven. That's about as serious a bug class as this project tracks. Fixed, with a proper fallback and a boot-time safety net, and the re-review came back clean.

Wave three was the engine capstone: runtime capability binding, plus a real-relay proof, not a mock -- a genuine challenge comes in, the policy is consulted, the response gets signed, authentication goes out, the relay says okay, and real rows come back. Challenge to rows, proven, plus denial-parking, a fresh challenge on reconnect, and a dedicated wrong-challenge oracle to prove the test would actually catch a broken implementation.

Wave five moved that onto the app-facing Rust interface: register an authentication policy, add an account and get back a real registration handle, remove an account by that handle -- which is also why the old pull request five twenty nine, with its cruder pubkey-only shape, got closed as superseded. Worth pausing on: doing it the naive way, re-exporting the engine's internal types, would have blown through this project's governed thirty-thousand-line facade ceiling. Instead every type got redefined fresh at the boundary. The public surface didn't grow to pay for this feature -- it shrank.

Wave six, merged this session, closed it out: the projection to Swift and Kotlin. An authentication-policy interface, a resolve-or-cancel completion object, per-session diagnostics, typed errors instead of vague failure reasons. Verdict: no correctness holes. Seven dedicated race tests around resolving versus cancelling, all held.

In practice: an iOS app, an Android app, a desktop app, can now register an authentication policy, respond to a relay's challenge, do authenticated reads and writes against a content relay, and read back per-session diagnostics. Proven against a real relay that enforces strict authentication, with a wrong-challenge oracle built so the proof can't be accused of testing nothing.

The soul of this arc is the discipline, not just the feature. Every one of the six waves got an independent adversarial review before it was allowed to merge -- reviewed like an adversary wrote it. And the governed public-surface ceiling was never bypassed once. When a wave needed more room, the team either deferred the surface to a later wave, or redesigned it to shrink instead.

A few other things landed this session worth naming. The read side got its own flagship: observe of a query and a window, replacing two overlapping read concepts with one, so a deep scroll now holds a constant number of live subscriptions per relay instead of leaking one every time you page forward. A batch of bounded-memory and indexing fixes closed out several unbounded bookkeeping structures across the store and router. And the store's page cache got an explicit sixty-four megabyte ceiling, cutting peak memory on a million-event ingest run by eighty-four percent.

The honest part, because that's the point of these reports. Two things remain open. Engine shutdown can still block forever if an app's own cancellation hook never returns -- an app-hook contract issue, not specific to authentication, and it's tracked. And there's a flaky macOS-only test in the ingest-smoke suite, tracked as infrastructure, not a product bug. Beyond those two, there is no known app-facing gap left in the authentication path itself.

So: NIP-42 came in through the front door this arc. Every wave reviewed like an adversary wrote it, and a governed public surface that never had to bend to get it done. That's the briefing. Thanks for listening.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-16 06:56 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

Last hour's headline was that content-relay authentication, N I P forty-two, shipped complete and issue eight closed. This hour has three real merges, and they tell two different stories: the project locking in its own review discipline, and the next epic starting to land.

First, the discipline story. Pull request five forty seven closes issue four ninety six, and it's worth taking seriously. There's a standing rule here that a bug class only really closes when a mechanism makes the bad path unreachable, not when a convention just says not to do it. Up to now, the architecture-review checklist -- checking whether a change introduces a new noun, breaks reachability, mishandles a boolean lifecycle, or touches a destructive A P I -- lived in a document a reviewer had to remember to open. Now it's a checked list right in the contributor guide, backed by two actual blocking continuous-integration jobs: cross-S D K parity, which fails if the Swift or Kotlin interface drifts from Rust, and falsifier honesty, which checks that a pull request's claimed updated falsifiers actually name real symbols and files. Backtested clean against eight recent merged pull requests and forty-three named claims, and it does catch a fabricated claim and a simulated version of the exact bug class that slipped through once before.

Second, the signer-lifecycle epic, issue forty-seven, just landed its headline unit. An application can now publish a single write under a registered secondary identity -- a podcast account, a disposable identity -- without touching the app's active account at all, across Rust, the foreign-function interface, Swift, and Kotlin. The proven part is the sharp one: once that write is accepted under the override identity, it is pinned there permanently. A later switch of the active account cannot retarget it to a different signer, even if that signer becomes available first -- proven directly, including across a real close-and-reopen of the store. Naming an identity with no attached signer yet just parks the write durably until the right key shows up. Right behind this, standard Keychain and Android Keystore secure-signer providers are staged and ready to build on top.

Smaller but real: the local key signer's secret now lives in a zeroizing byte buffer the compiler fences to wipe on drop, with its debug output redacted to just the public key -- the first landed unit of this same epic, from a session or two back.

Two things just got underway, worth naming honestly because neither has shipped code yet: a Blossom media module, authenticated blob upload with sha-two-fifty-six content addressing, has its scoping issue opened; and a consolidated architecture decision record landed, fifteen rulings against this project's own standing doctrine.

One unchanged note: the flaky macOS-only ingest-smoke test is still just that, tracked infrastructure, not a product bug.

That's the pulse -- the project enforcing its own review rules by machine, and the post-authentication push, secure signer lifecycle first, genuinely underway.

title: NMP Briefing — 2026-07-16 07:53 UTC
text:
Hey, it's the NMP briefing. This one's the deeper cut, so let's take the long view instead of just this hour's diff.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch. Everything in it collapses to two nouns: a live query for reading, and a write intent for publishing. Every arc this project has walked lately is really just teaching those two nouns to handle a harder case honestly, instead of quietly adding a third concept.

Case one: content-relay authentication, N I P forty-two, walked all the way through last arc. Relay identity stopped being a bare U R L and became a session tied to who's actually asking. A challenge-response reducer got built and adversarially reviewed hard enough to catch a real missed-wakeup bug before it shipped. The whole thing got proven end to end against a real relay before it ever reached an app. Then it crossed the Rust facade and the Swift and Kotlin projections, closing issue eight for good. That's the write-intent noun learning to carry an authenticated identity — not a new noun bolted on beside it.

Case two, the one still closing out: issue forty-seven, signer lifecycle. Same shape, harder edges. First a zeroize-hardening pass, so a local key's secret lives in memory that's wiped on drop instead of an ordinary byte array. Then per-write identity override — publish one thing under a different registered identity without touching the app's active account, proven immune to a later account switch even across a store close and reopen. This cycle, the piece staged behind that landed too: real platform-backed secure vaults, a Keychain-backed store on Swift and a Java Key Store-backed store on Kotlin, so a session restores itself securely instead of living in a plaintext development file. Three of four units done.

Now the honest part, because that's the point of these reports. The last unit — carrying the exact frozen identity on a parked write, so an app can see whose signature it's still waiting on, not just that something's stuck — is sitting in continuous integration right now, and as of this recording it's failing. Not a flaky test. Its own cross-surface check caught direct Rust and the foreign-function projection reporting two genuinely different frozen identities for the same parked write. That's the adversarial-review discipline this project keeps talking about actually doing its job: catching a real bug before merge instead of after. It is not landed, and it doesn't get counted as landed until it's fixed and green.

Case three, just getting started: Blossom, blob and media storage. A brand-new Rust crate now builds and verifies upload authorization — a signed grant naming exactly one blob by its content hash, so a server can trust the upload without trusting the uploader in general. That's still the write-intent shape underneath: an authorization is just a specialized signed event, and verification is just a durable acceptance check wearing a new hat. What's missing is everything past upload — mirroring a blob to a second server, deleting one you own, listing what a pubkey has stored — plus the entire foreign-function, Swift, and Kotlin projection. Direct Rust only, today. No app can call this yet.

So here's the honest state of embedding NMP right now. Content-relay authentication is real and shippable end to end, Rust through Swift and Kotlin. Signer lifecycle is three-quarters there, with its last unit's own review net doing exactly what it was built to do. Blossom media has its first honest brick laid, with real scaffolding still ahead of it. Same two nouns underneath all three. Same discipline. Just a harder case each time. That's the briefing. Thanks for listening.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-16 08:58 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

Big news this cycle: issue forty-seven, the signer-lifecycle epic, is fully closed. Last time, the final unit was sitting in continuous integration with its own parity test failing, correctly, having caught direct-Rust and the foreign-function interface reporting two different frozen identities for the same parked write. That fix landed clean. So all four units are now merged across Rust, the foreign-function interface, Swift, and Kotlin: the local key signer's secret lives in memory that gets wiped on drop; an app can publish a single write under a registered secondary identity without touching its active account, and that identity can never be silently retargeted later, even across a restart; a reattached write now carries the exact pubkey it's still waiting on, not just a vague still-parked status; and both platforms ship a proper secure vault -- Keychain on Swift, Java KeyStore on Kotlin -- so a session restores itself automatically instead of relying on a plaintext development file. If you're embedding NMP and juggling more than one identity, or you care about a session surviving an app restart without stashing a raw key on disk, that whole story is done.

Second story this cycle: Blossom, the media and blob-storage module, kept moving. Mirror, delete, and list all landed on top of the upload-authorization core that shipped earlier -- each one its own bound authorization, so a server can trust exactly the one operation it's asked to perform, not a blanket credential. That's the whole BUD-eleven verb set now proven on the Rust side.

Now the honest part, because that's the point of these reports. The next piece -- projecting all of that out through the foreign-function interface to Swift and Kotlin, so an app can actually call it -- is sitting in a pull request right now, and it is not green. The Rust tests pass, cross-software-development-kit parity passes, the falsifier-honesty check passes, the surface-governance check passes. But the Swift package build itself fails: a real type mismatch, a raw byte array being passed where the wrapper expects Apple's Data type. That's not a flaky test or infrastructure noise -- it's a genuine compile error in the generated wrapper, caught before merge instead of after. So: Blossom's core verbs are solid direct-Rust today, but there's still no app-facing blob-upload call on any platform until that fix lands.

Put together: the signer story closes clean this cycle, and the media story is one honest bug fix away from reaching the app layer.

That's the pulse.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-16 09:51 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

Last cycle left one honest loose end. Blossom, the media and blob-storage module, had every verb working on the Rust side, but the projection out to Swift and Kotlin was sitting in a pull request with a real compile failure -- a raw byte array where the generated wrapper expected Apple's Data type.

That's fixed now, and it's fixed for real, not just re-flaked. The pull request landed clean: Rust tests, cross-software-development-kit parity, the falsifier-honesty check, and both the Swift and Kotlin package builds are all green. Blob upload, mirror, delete, and list -- the whole BUD-eleven verb set -- is now callable from Swift and from Kotlin, not only from Rust.

Here's what that actually unlocks. Before this, if you were embedding NMP in a native app and wanted a user to attach a photo to a post, there was nothing to call from the app layer -- the machinery existed, but only in Rust. Now an iOS app or an Android app can build an upload authorization, hand a file to the client, and get back a verified, content-addressed blob reference, with the same typed error handling on every platform. That's a real app-facing capability today, not a future promise.

Now, the honest part, because that's the point of these reports. Uploading a blob today is a standalone asynchronous call. It is not yet a durable, engine-owned obligation the way a Nostr write intent is. If your app crashes mid-upload, or the network drops, retrying that upload is on you, the app, not on the engine. The team actually made a deliberate call here: rather than hold this feature back until uploads get full crash-durable retry, they scoped that as a separate, additive upgrade for later, filed as its own tracked issue, and shipped the useful, callable version now. So: posting a photo is crash-durable on the Nostr-publish half today, but not yet on the upload half. That gap is named and open, not hidden.

What's still ahead for this epic: wiring picture-event metadata, so an uploaded blob gets properly described as a NIP-sixty-eight picture post, and a composition layer that ties upload and publish together into one clean flow. Both are scoped and queued, not started yet.

So: Blossom media just crossed the platform line. Rust, Swift, and Kotlin can all call it today. The durability upgrade is a known, named, deliberately deferred next step, not a corner quietly cut.

That's the pulse.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-16 10:51 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

Two things worth talking about this cycle. First, a quiet fix landed: the NIP-sixty-eight picture-event builder now takes an explicit timestamp instead of grabbing the clock at build time. Sounds small, but it closes a real determinism gap -- the same picture draft built twice, or built once in Rust and once through the foreign-function-interface boundary, now produces byte-identical output. That matters more than it sounds like, because the parity checks across Rust, Swift, and Kotlin depend on exactly that kind of determinism.

Now the bigger arc. There's a pull request open right now for what the team's calling the media composition seam -- stitching a blob upload to Blossom and a Nostr publish into one coherent flow, instead of leaving an app to hand-wire the two together. And the interesting design decision here isn't the happy path, it's the failure path.

Uploading a photo and publishing a note are two genuinely separate systems talking to two different kinds of servers. So what happens when one succeeds and the other doesn't? The design keeps those as three distinct, separated outcomes, by construction, not by convention. The upload can fail on its own, cleanly, before anything Nostr-related is even attempted. The publish can fail on its own, after the upload already succeeded -- meaning your app is left holding a perfectly good, verified blob reference it can retry publishing without re-uploading the file. Or both succeed, and you get a real picture event. What the design explicitly refuses to do is fold those into one ambiguous "did it work" boolean. If your upload succeeded and your publish didn't, you get told that precisely, not a generic failure.

Right now that composition pull request is sitting in continuous integration -- Rust tests, cross-software-development-kit parity, and the falsifier-honesty check are running. It hasn't merged yet, so I'm not going to claim it's done. It's real, it's scoped, and it's close.

One more thing worth flagging, separate from the code: the project's fifteen architecture rulings -- decisions the team made about where the v2 surface is heading -- now have a public escalation page with audio for each one. There's an overall briefing plus a deep-dive recording per ruling, so if you've ever wondered why a particular application-programming-interface shape was chosen, you can actually go listen to the reasoning instead of just reading a changelog line.

That's the pulse.

title: NMP Briefing — 2026-07-16 11:56 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP briefing. This one's the deep cut -- let's step back from any single hour and look at how the pieces now fit together.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch. Everything in it reduces to two nouns. A live query, for reading -- you describe what you want, and the engine keeps a local view current and hands you real evidence, never a fake "it's synced" boolean. And a write intent, for publishing -- a durable obligation the engine carries through signing, routing, and retry, telling you exactly what happened, not a false global success.

Every arc this project walks is really teaching those same two nouns to handle a harder case honestly, instead of quietly growing a third concept. Tonight's throughline: authentication, identity, and now media.

Start with authentication. Nostr relays used to be just a U R L you connected to. Issue eight taught the write intent, and the live query, that a relay connection can be a session tied to who's actually asking. Six adversarially-reviewed waves took this from concept to production: relay identity keyed by session instead of raw address, a proper AUTH reducer with challenge epochs, a real proof against an actual challenging relay -- challenge, sign, AUTH, OK, then rows flow -- and finally the whole thing projected through the foreign-function interface to Swift and Kotlin. Worth remembering: the team did all of this under its own thirty-thousand-line governed surface ceiling, without growing the guardrail to fit the feature. That arc is now closed, completely, end to end.

Second arc: signer lifecycle, issue forty-seven. If authentication taught the connection who's asking, this arc taught the signing path which identity is asking, and how to protect it. Four units landed. A local key's secret now lives in memory that's compiler-fenced to wipe on drop. An app can publish one write under a secondary registered identity -- a podcast account, a disposable identity -- without touching its main active account, and once accepted under that override, a later account switch can never silently retarget it to a different signer, even across a real close-and-reopen of the store. A parked write waiting on a signer now carries the exact public key it's waiting on, not a vague "still stuck" -- and the review process caught a real bug here, direct-Rust and the foreign-function interface reporting two different frozen identities for the same write, before it reached users. And real platform-backed vaults -- Keychain on Swift, Java KeyStore on Kotlin -- restore a session automatically instead of a plaintext development file. Put together: an app can juggle more than one identity safely, survive a restart without a raw key on disk, and never worry about a stuck write jumping to the wrong signer.

Now the arc moving fastest lately: Blossom, the media and blob-storage module, issue two-sixteen. Four stories stacked into one capability.

Story one: upload authorization -- a signed grant naming exactly one blob by its content hash, so a server trusts an upload without trusting the uploader in general. Same write-intent shape, wearing a media hat. Story two: the rest of the verb set -- mirror a blob to a second server, delete one you own, list what a public key has stored -- each with its own narrowly bound authorization, never a blanket credential. Story three: all of that projected through the foreign-function interface, so a real iOS or Android app can call upload, mirror, delete, and list today, with matching typed errors on every platform.

Story four just landed: NIP-sixty-eight picture events, plus the seam tying it together. A new crate, nmp-media, wires prepare, upload, and compose into three separate stages, and the design choice is the whole point. Uploading a photo to Blossom and publishing a note to Nostr are two different systems talking to two different kinds of servers. What happens when one succeeds and the other fails? The naive answer is one ambiguous boolean. NMP's answer is three separate, typed outcomes, by construction. The upload can fail cleanly before anything Nostr-related is attempted. The publish can fail after the upload already succeeded -- your app keeps a verified blob reference it can retry publishing without re-uploading the file. Or both succeed, and you get a real picture event. That seam merged this cycle, completing the upload-through-publish arc in Rust.

So here's what an app can build end to end today: authenticated relay sessions, safe multi-identity signing that survives a restart, and a full photo-posting flow -- upload, verify, describe as a picture event, publish -- every failure named precisely, never smoothed into one status flag.

Now the honest part. Two things stay open, on purpose. Upload durability is still app-owned -- if your app crashes mid-upload, retrying is on you; an engine-integrated durable upload is a deliberate later addition, not a hidden gap. And the foreign-function projection of this newest media work, plus NIP-sixty-eight itself, is still Rust-only -- Swift and Kotlin access is coming, batched together, but hasn't shipped. Separately, brand-new work just started, not merged: pulling content parsing apart from which component owns acquiring a Nostr reference. Three draft pull requests are open against it; none have landed.

One more loose end: a flaky macOS-only test I've mentioned before, in the ingest-smoke suite, is fixed now -- two real socket races in the test's own mock server, not a product bug, but good to close.

Same two nouns the whole way through. Authentication taught the connection who's asking. Signer lifecycle taught it which identity, safely. Blossom taught it how to carry media, with failure kept honest instead of collapsed. That's the briefing. Thanks for listening.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-16 12:53 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

Two reliability stories this cycle, and they're both the unglamorous kind -- the sort that never show up on a feature list, but change whether you can actually trust the thing day to day.

First: a macOS-only test flake in the ingest-smoke suite finally got root-caused, not just re-run until it went green. It turned out to be a real socket subtlety: when a mock server's listening socket is set non-blocking, on Linux a freshly accepted client connection does not inherit that flag -- but on macOS and B-S-D, it does. So the test would occasionally see a connection that was already non-blocking when its own logic assumed otherwise, producing a connection reset that had nothing to do with the actual relay-ingest code under test. Two of those races were in the test's own mock server, not in NMP itself, and both are fixed now; the flake, open long enough to earn its own tracked issue, is closed. Why it matters even though it's "just a test": a flaky test trains everyone to start ignoring red builds, and an ignored red build is exactly how a real regression sneaks through later.

Second, and this one's app-facing: queries can now carry an explicit freshness policy. Before this, every observation did the same thing every time -- check the cache, then go live and hold a subscription open, even if you'd asked for the exact same data seconds ago and nothing had changed. Now a query can ask for max-age, a plain "don't bother hitting the network if the local cache is already fresh enough," or cache-only, which never touches the network and just tells you what's already on disk. Both are answered directly from coverage watermarks NMP already tracks per query -- no new polling loop, no new background job, just honest reuse of bookkeeping the engine already had.

What that means if you're embedding NMP: your background refresh, your pull-to-refresh, your "did anything actually change" check can now say exactly how fresh they're willing to accept, instead of either hammering the network every time or hand-rolling your own staleness cache on top of the engine. And the tests proving relay ingest actually works just got more honest, not more lenient.

One more thing, landing right as this cycle wrapped: writes can now be explicitly cancelled before they're signed. If your app accepts a post but the user backs out before it ever reaches a signer, there's now a real, durable cancel call on every platform -- not dropping a handle and hoping, an actual typed outcome that either succeeds durably or tells you precisely why it can't.

That's the pulse.

title: NMP Pulse — 2026-07-16 13:53 UTC
text:
Hey, this is the NMP pulse.

Quick refresher: NMP is an embeddable Nostr client engine, a Rust core with Swift and Kotlin software development kits, that owns relay handling, event storage, and durable publishing so your app doesn't have to rebuild all of that from scratch.

This cycle's throughline is reliability under pressure -- two stories, one lesson: never let something you can't control turn into unbounded growth or unrecoverable state.

First story: bounded delivery for a slow reader. Every live query in NMP hands you results as they change, but what happens if your app is busy -- a slow render, a paused view, a background tab -- and can't keep up with how fast the network produces updates? Before this fix, the channel feeding that query was an ordinary unbounded queue. Every skipped update sat there waiting, so a slow consumer paired with a fast relay could mean unbounded memory growth, plus replaying a pile of stale intermediate states once your app caught up. The fix replaces that queue with a single slot. When a new update arrives and the last one hasn't been picked up yet, the engine doesn't queue it behind the old one -- it merges them into one exact transition, rebased on top of whatever your app last actually saw. So when your app does catch up, it gets the current truth in one hop, not a backlog of everything it missed. Memory is now bounded by the gap between what you've seen and what's true right now, never by how many updates arrived while you weren't looking.

Second story, landing right alongside it: writes can now be explicitly cancelled before they're signed. Your app accepts a post the moment the user hits send -- that's how NMP gives you a responsive local view immediately. But then the user backs out, or the content changes, before that post reaches a signer. Until now, there was no clean, durable way to say "actually, forget that one." Now there is: a real cancel call, keyed to the exact receipt for that write, on every platform -- Rust, the foreign function interface, Swift, and Kotlin. Call it, and if the write hasn't signed yet, the engine atomically undoes the optimistic local row, restores whatever it displaced, and writes a durable cancelled fact to disk in the same transaction, so a crash right after cancelling can't resurrect the write. If the write already crossed the signature boundary, you get a precise typed refusal instead of a silent no-op -- your app never has to guess whether the cancel actually took effect.

Put those two together and here's what it means if you're the one embedding NMP. Your memory footprint under a flood of updates is now predictable -- a slow screen doesn't turn into a leak. And your write path finally has an honest undo button for the one window where undo is actually possible: before the signature exists. Both are typed, both are durable, and both went through adversarial review before merging -- the cancellation work in particular caught and fixed real bugs around signer-task leaks and quarantined writes during review, not after it shipped.

That's the pulse.
